2005 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival to include US Championships

The 2005 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival will begin with the Western States Orienteering Championships in Cle Elum (hosted by COC) and end with the US Individual Championships in Bend, Oregon (hosted by ORCA). In between, orienteers will be able to participate in a sprint adventure race, a long sprint orienteering course, and a relay. There will also be a junior training camp.
The current PNWOF2005 schedule follows:

__PH_0__ - Western States Championships, Cle Elum, WA (Cascade O' Club) August 22 (Mon) - short adventure race with orienteering-only option, Cle Elum, WA (COC)
__PH_2__ - travel day August 24-25 (Wed-Thu) - junior training camp, Bend
__PH_4__ - long sprint (prologue + chase), Arnold Ice Cave, Bend, OR (US Team) August 25 (Thu) - relay, Lava Butte, Bend, OR (US Team)
__PH_6__ - model event, Dutchman Flats, Bend, OR (ORCA) August 27-28 (Sat/Sun) - US Championships, Bend, OR (ORCA)

This should be a great series of events, so keep up to date by regularly checking the PNWOF website (www.pnwof.org).

Mapping Plans

In preparation for PNWOF 2005, we recently updated our maps in the Cle Elum area. Cle Elum Ridge and Teanaway Forks were updated by Mark Dominie, a mapper from New York. In addition, we have a new base map for Lord Hill Park produced by Mapping Coordinator Pat Kelly, which we plan to use for a field-checking excursion this fall/winter.

School League

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL) begins its 23rd season on November 6 at Woodland Park in Seattle. The school league is a series of orienteering events aimed at school-age kids, culminating in a championship event in February. The events are open to kids from 1st through 12th grade, with individual and team awards. Please visit our website for more details about WIOL and to download a registration form.

Winter O' Series

The 2004-2005 edition of the Winter O' Series begins on November 6. The O' Series consists of the longest course at each WIOL event (see above) and is designed for adults. The O' Series Championship is also held in conjunction with the WIOL championship in February. Pre-registration for the O' Series is not required; just do the longest course at an O' Series event and you are automatically entered in the series. Points are awarded based on your placing and your highest 4 scores are added to get your total O' Series score. Awards are given to the top 3 men and top 3 women for the season and for the championship.

Annual Meeting and Board of Directors Elections

This year's annual meeting will take place on November 20 following the orienteering event at Woodland Park. Meet at 1:30 at Olympia Pizza (4501 Interlake Ave. N., 7 blocks from Woodland Park) for food (buy your own) and socializing.

You should be receiving a ballot in the mail shortly to elect 5 board members. You can either mail the ballot back or turn it in at the annual meeting. Election results will be announced at the annual meeting.

Following the annual meeting, the board will hold its monthly meeting, which everyone is welcome to attend.

Frequent O' Card

Ever arrived at an orienteering event and found you had no cash, thus requiring you to borrow money from your friends? You can purchase a Frequent O' Card, a pre-paid punchcard accepted at any Cascade event. As a bonus, after you use all the punches, turn in the card for a free $5 entry. Cards are $50 each and have 10 punch boxes worth $5 each. To get a punch card, send a check for $50 and a self-addressed, stamped envelope to:

Cascade Orienteering Club
P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, WA 98103

You can also purchase the cards at events from Debbie Newell (dnewel@verizon.net, 425-488-3691). Please contact her before an event to ensure that she will have the cards at that event.

Jim Siscel Visits 172 Ballparks

!Headshot of Jim Siscel, smiling at the camera wearing glasses

Cascade's membership coordinator, Jim Siscel, and his wife Andrea, recently completed a tour of all 172 major- and minor-league baseball parks in the US and Canada. The Siscels split the tour into 3 seasons, beginning in June 2002 and ending in August of this year.

The story of the Siscels' odyssey, along with their picture, appeared on the front page of the Seattle Times, and Jim was interviewed by several radio and newspaper reporters.

If you'd like more details about the Siscels' trip, check out their web page at www.baseballroadtrip.net.

Jim has now returned to his humble existence as COC's membership coordinator, but would probably agree to an autograph if asked.

Bresemans Take National Relay Championship

by Rick Breseman

[Following is Rick Breseman's blow-by-blow account of the US Relay Championships held in Maine in September. The Bresemans won the 12-point category.]

Ryan led off and finished ahead of all other runners, giving us a 3½-minute lead.

Kelsey took the second leg. Our strongest competitors opted to put their strongest runner (Tony Federer from UNO) on this leg and he managed to catch and pass Kelsey. However, Kelsey became inspired and outsprinted him on the run to the finish.

!The Breseman clan running together in a grassy field. From left to right: Kelsey, Rick, Ryan, and Eileen wearing matching white relay jerseys.
The Breseman clan (from left): Kelsey, Rick, Ryan, and Eileen

Eileen, therefore, started with a slim lead on the 3rd leg. She was passed by Deb Humisten of UNO on the way to the first control, but maintained her calm and gained back the lead and then some.

So, when I started the anchor leg, I had a good lead over the next official team, but a slim lead over a strong unofficial team anchored by Jim Eagleton. Jim ran well and passed me several times when I hesitated or miscued a couple of times. It took me until the last quarter of the race to be sure that all competitors were behind - Jim was a close second and the next official team about 7 minutes back.

Balsamroot

by Joe Meuchel

!A large cluster of Balsamroot flowers - bright yellow, sunflower-like blooms growing out of rocky soil in Eastern Washington
Balsamwood in Eastern Washington

Not many plants can put on a flower show like our balsamroot. This sunny sunflower with large arrow-shaped basal leaves can and does turn hillsides into a blaze of yellow-orange. You just can't possibly miss these.

An aptly named plant, this showy plant provided nourishment for Native Americans from pre-emergence to post flowering.

In early spring when the soil begins to warm, the balsamroot begins to produce fiddle-necked curled shoots below the surface. Root gatherers, hungry for a taste of something green, would hunt up last year's telltale curled dried leaves and dig up the newly-formed shoots.

Later, the bud shoots, young leaves, and peeled balsam tasting roots were also utilized. The plant's largesse didn't end there, but furnished yet another staple: when ripe, gatherers with a stick and a basket would gather the mature seeds to eat raw, or ground into flour with mortar and pestle. The Yakama Indians have 6 different names for this plant, depending on its stage of development.

The plant was so plentiful in some areas of the west that early pioneers utilized it also: they called the flour Pinole and baked it into Mormon Biscuits.

Submitted by Don Atkinson. Originally printed in the Cascades Mountain-Echo (May 2003).

Rattlesnake Lake North Bend, June 12

About 50 newcomers joined us at Rattlesnake Lake on Mountains to Sound Greenway Discovery Days. It seems that most of them learned about the event through the Discovery Days publicity. A few regulars came, also, to check out the new map completed by Mike Schuh and Eric Bone. The park was quite busy; there was also a multi-sport race that involved at least paddling and biking, and several participants thought our registration table was a race checkpoint. A few casual passers-by decided to do a course, and one Swedish-American fellow stopped in just to pick up a Cascade event schedule.

Eric Bone set up the event with the Discovery Days people and set the courses. Many thanks to Mike Schuh for clinics and to Libby & Don Dermenstein for registration, start, and finish. We look forward to having more meets in this beautiful lakeside green space.

2005 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival to include US Championships

The 2005 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival will begin with the Western States Orienteering Championships in Cle Elum (hosted by COC) and end with the US Individual Championships in Bend, Oregon (hosted by ORCA). In between, orienteers will be able to participate in a sprint adventure race, a long sprint orienteering course, and a relay. There will also be a junior training camp. The current PNWOF2005 schedule follows:

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (WIOL) begins its 23rd season on November 6 at Woodland Park in Seattle. The school league is a series of orienteering events aimed at school-age kids, culminating in a championship event in February. The events are open to kids from 1st through 12th grade, with individual and team awards. Please visit our website for more details about WIOL and to download a registration form.

The 2004-2005 edition of the Winter O' Series begins on November 6. The O' Series consists of the longest course at each WIOL event (see above) and is designed for adults. The O' Series Championship is also held in conjunction with the WIOL championship in February. Pre-registration for the O' Series is not required; just do the longest course at an O' Series event and you are automatically entered in the series. Points are awarded based on your placing and your highest 4 scores are added to get your total O' Series score. Awards are given to the top 3 men and top 3 women for the season and for the championship.

[Following is Rick Breseman's blow-by-blow account of the US Relay Championships held in Maine in September. The Bresemans won the 12-point category.]
Ryan led off and finished ahead of all other runners, giving us a 3½-minute lead.

Kelsey took the second leg. Our strongest competitors opted to put their strongest runner (Tony Federer from UNO) on this leg and he managed to catch and pass Kelsey. However, Kelsey became inspired and outsprinted him on the run to the finish.

!The Breseman clan running together in a grassy field. From left to right: Kelsey, Rick, Ryan, and Eileen wearing matching white relay jerseys. The Breseman clan (from left): Kelsey, Rick, Ryan, and Eileen
Eileen, therefore, started with a slim lead on the 3rd leg. She was passed by Deb Humisten of UNO on the way to the first control, but maintained her calm and gained back the lead and then some.

So, when I started the anchor leg, I had a good lead over the next official team, but a slim lead over a strong unofficial team anchored by Jim Eagleton. Jim ran well and passed me several times when I hesitated or miscued a couple of times. It took me until the last quarter of the race to be sure that all competitors were behind - Jim was a close second and the next official team about 7 minutes back.
!A large cluster of Balsamroot flowers - bright yellow, sunflower-like blooms growing out of rocky soil in Eastern Washington Balsamwood in Eastern Washington

Not many plants can put on a flower show like our balsamroot. This sunny sunflower with large arrow-shaped basal leaves can and does turn hillsides into a blaze of yellow-orange. You just can't possibly miss these.
An aptly named plant, this showy plant provided nourishment for Native Americans from pre-emergence to post flowering.

In early spring when the soil begins to warm, the balsamroot begins to produce fiddle-necked curled shoots below the surface. Root gatherers, hungry for a taste of something green, would hunt up last year's telltale curled dried leaves and dig up the newly-formed shoots.

Later, the bud shoots, young leaves, and peeled balsam tasting roots were also utilized. The plant's largesse didn't end there, but furnished yet another staple: when ripe, gatherers with a stick and a basket would gather the mature seeds to eat raw, or ground into flour with mortar and pestle. The Yakama Indians have 6 different names for this plant, depending on its stage of development.
The plant was so plentiful in some areas of the west that early pioneers utilized it also: they called the flour Pinole and baked it into Mormon Biscuits.
